coredata.dc:title: Dual emission chiral carbon dots as fluorescent probe for fast chiral recognition of tryptophan enantiomers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qi
Chen, Yuan
Wang, Yan-Li
Song, Ji-Ying
Lv, Hai-Tao
Sun, Ya-Ming
coredata.dc:description:
Background Chirality is an essential property of nature. Chiral recognition is of great significance to life sciences, pharmaceutical industry, food analysis, and so on. Chiral carbon dots (CCDs), as green nanomaterials, have great prospects in chiral sensing. However, CCDs with enantioselectivity for tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers are scarce. Moreover, most chiral sensing platforms depend on the difference of fluorescence intensity at the same emission wavelength to identify enantiomers, it is still a challenge to distinguish enantiomers by the positions of fluorescent emission peaks.
Results Novel CCDs with specific chiral recognition ability for Trp enantiomers are synthesized using l-lysine and l-cysteine as precursors. The CCDs have two fluorescent emission peaks at 390 nm and 450 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of CCDs at 390 nm enhances obviously on the addition of L-Trp, while it enhances slightly at 450 nm in the presence of D-Trp. This chiral sensing system not only can identify Trp enantiomers according to fluorescence intensity, but also achieves the distinguishment depending on emission wavelength. The enantioselectivity (IL/ID) reaches 4.5 when the concentration of Trp enantiomer is 1 mM. This chiral sensing platform not only can be used for quantitative analysis of D-Trp and L-Trp, but also can be used for determining the enantiomeric excess of racemates. The chiral recognition mechanism is investigated by molecular simulation. It is found that L-Trp has higher binding energy with CCDs.
Significance This work presents a novel kind of CCDs with special chiral recognition performance for Trp enantiomers, and opens the door to identify chiral isomers according to wavelength difference, which has profound significance for the development of chiral sensing platforms, and may provide inspirations for the design of novel CCDs with excellent chiral recognition performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dots
Chiral recognition
Tryptophan
Enantiomers
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pretreatment and detection of real samples
Enantioselectivity of CCDs for Trp enantiomers
Specific chiral recognition ability for Trp enantiomers
Comparison of this method with others

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CCDs (a); FTIR spectrum of CCDs (b); XPS survey of CCDs (c); XPS C1s spectrum of CCDs (d); XPS O1s spectrum of CCDs (e); XPS N1s spectrum of CCDs (f); XPS S2p spectrum of CCDs (g); Circular dichroism spectra of CCDs, L-Lys and L-Cys (h); UV–vis absorption spectrum of CCDs (i).
Fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs under different excitation wavelengths (a); Fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs in the presence of different concentrations of L-Trp or D-Trp (b).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs in the presence of different concentrations of L-Trp (a); Fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs in the presence of different concentrations of D-Trp (b); Standard curves of I–I0 versus the concentrations of L-Trp or D-Trp (c); Fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs in the presence of racemates with different percentages of L-Trp (d); Standard curves of I390–I450 versus the percentages of L-Trp (e); Enantioselectivity of CCDs for 19 chiral amino acids (f).

The molecular docking models of D-/L-Trp and CCDs (the green dashed line represents hydrogen bond, and the number represents the distance, Unit: Å). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of fluorescent chiral recognition methods for Trp enantiomers.

row.entry: L-amino acid oxidase (LAAO)/Horseradish peroxidase (HRP)/zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) composite
Chiral poly(ionic liquid) in the presence of Cu(II)
Chiral supramolecular assemblies of CdTe quantum dots and CTAB

head.ce:title: Dual emission chiral carbon dots as fluorescent probe for fast chiral recognition of tryptophan enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory/Key Laboratory of Henan Province, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory/Key Laboratory of Henan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Laboratory/Key Laboratory of Henan Province, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chirality is an essential property of nature. Chiral recognition is of great significance to life sciences, pharmaceutical industry, food analysis, and so on. Chiral carbon dots (CCDs), as green nanomaterials, have great prospects in chiral sensing. However, CCDs with enantioselectivity for tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers are scarce. Moreover, most chiral sensing platforms depend on the difference of fluorescence intensity at the same emission wavelength to identify enantiomers, it is still a challenge to distinguish enantiomers by the positions of fluorescent emission peaks.

Novel CCDs with specific chiral recognition ability for Trp enantiomers are synthesized using l-lysine and l-cysteine as precursors. The CCDs have two fluorescent emission peaks at 390 nm and 450 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of CCDs at 390 nm enhances obviously on the addition of L-Trp, while it enhances slightly at 450 nm in the presence of D-Trp. This chiral sensing system not only can identify Trp enantiomers according to fluorescence intensity, but also achieves the distinguishment depending on emission wavelength. The enantioselectivity (IL/ID) reaches 4.5 when the concentration of Trp enantiomer is 1 mM. This chiral sensing platform not only can be used for quantitative analysis of D-Trp and L-Trp, but also can be used for determining the enantiomeric excess of racemates. The chiral recognition mechanism is investigated by molecular simulation. It is found that L-Trp has higher binding energy with CCDs.

This work presents a novel kind of CCDs with special chiral recognition performance for Trp enantiomers, and opens the door to identify chiral isomers according to wavelength difference, which has profound significance for the development of chiral sensing platforms, and may provide inspirations for the design of novel CCDs with excellent chiral recognition performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is an essential feature of nature. Many biomolecules such as amino acids, sugars, proteins and DNA have chirality. Chiral molecules always have the same chemical structure but different physiological functions. For example, L-amino acids can be used for synthesizing proteins in human body, but D-amino acids can not [1]. Chiral recognition of amino acids enantiomers is of great significance to understand the molecular recognition mechanism in biological systems, and is helpful to accurate disease diagnosis and medical treatment, which has gained more and more research attention [2,3].

L-Tryptophan (L-Trp) is an essential amino acid, should be supplemented from food to maintain human health. L-Trp has important physiological functions, such as it is the precursor of 5-hydroxytryptamine which is an important neurotransmitter in human body [4,5]. It is difficult to accurately identify D-Trp and L-Trp because of their same physicochemical properties except for different optical activity. At present, methods such as chromatography [6,7], circular dichroism [8,9], electrochemistry [10–12], mass spectrum [13,14] and surface plasmon resonance [15] have made great contributions to identify Trp enantiomers, but also encounter problems such as complicated sample pretreatment, complex mechanism and operation, time consuming, low sensitivity and poor repeatability. Fluorescent chiral recognition methods, especially those based on fluorescent nanoparticles, are increasingly explored by scientists in recent years because of high sensitivity, simplicity and rapidity [16,17]. Basu and coworkers reported ligands-stabilized gold nanoclusters for sensitive chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers based on photoluminescence [18]. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity was quenched to a larger extent in the presence of D-Trp than that of L-Trp. Fahimi-Kashani and coworkers constructed a chiral recognition platform for discriminating Trp enantiomers using chiral supramolecular assemblies of CdTe quantum dots [19]. It was found that the fluorescence emission was quenched, also accompanied by an alteration of emission color in the presence of D-Trp, while L-Trp caused slight spectral changes. Above research opens up a new way for nanoparticles-based chiral sensing, but metal nanomaterials are not environment-friendly, their potential toxicity limits the applications in biological systems.

Carbon dots are carbon-based nanoparticles with good biocompatibility, low toxicity and excellent photoluminescence performance, have been widely applied in biosensing and bioimaging [20]. However, chiral carbon dots (CCDs) are relatively scarce since their formation mechanism are not clear. In 2019, CCDs synthesized by functionalizing carbon quantum dots with l-cysteine (L-Cys) were first used for solution phase sensing and nanopaper sensing towards L-Lys [21]. Later, CCDs synthesized by citric acid and L-aspartic acid were applied to sense L-Lys using Sn2+ as medium [22]. The fluorescence of CCDs could be quenched by Sn2+ and restored by L-Lys, while no obvious luminescence restore was observed upon addition of D-Lys. Ionized CCDs were synthesized for enantioselective discrimination of nonaromatic amino alcohols using Cu2+ as medium [23]. We synthesized CCDs using citric acid and L-Cys as precursors for distinguishing isoleucine (Ile) enantiomers [24]. Without any metal ions as indicators, the fluorescence intensity enhanced obviously in the presence of L-Ile, while almost unchanged in the presence of D-Ile. Recently, N,Cl-doped CCDs were synthesized using a natural deep eutectic solvent as the solvent, doping agent, and chiral source [25]. The N,Cl-doped CCDs exhibited great enantioselective recognition ability towards Lys enantiomers reflected by the change in fluorescence intensity. According to our best knowledge, CCDs with enantioselectivity towards Trp enantiomers have not been developed. Moreover, most of the chiral recognition methods depend on the fluorescence intensity difference at the same emission wavelength to identify enantiomers, it is still a challenge to distinguish enantiomers by the positions of fluorescent emission peak.

In this work, novel CCDs are synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis method using L-Cys and L-Lys as precursors. The CCDs exhibit good fluorescence property with two emission peaks at 390 nm and 450 nm. The chiral recognition can be observed by different fluorescence intensity change at 390 nm and 450 nm induced by L-Trp and D-Trp, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of CCDs at 390 nm enhances obviously in the presence of L-Trp, while enhances slightly at 450 nm in the presence of D-Trp. It should be noted that differently from previously reported methods in which enantioselectivity is based on fluorescence signal difference at the same emission wavelength, this chiral sensing platform achieves the distinguishment of Trp enantiomers by directly observing the change of fluorescence intensity at different emission wavelengths, which is more intuitive and effective.

Amino acids isomers including D-/L-Cys, D-/L-Lys, D-/L-Trp, D-/L-Ala, D-/L-Glu, D-/L-Thr, D-/L-Met, D-/L-Leu, D-/L-Ile, D-/L-Val, D-/L-Pro, D-/L-Arg, D-/L-His, D-/L-Phe, D-/L-Ser, D-/L-Gln, D-/L-Asn, D-/L-Asp, D-/L-Tyr are purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium phosphate and sodium phosphate monobasic are obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were captured by JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed on Zetasizer Nano ZSE nanoparticle size analyzer (Malvern, Britain). Circular dichroisms spectra were obtained on Chirascan circular dichroisms spectrometer (Applied Photophysics, Britain). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on VG ESCALAB 210 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Ultraviolet absorption spectrum was measured on V-3900 UV–visible spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence spectrum was obtained on F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Chromatographic data were obtained on Primaide HPLC system equipped with a manual injector with 20 μL sample loop, a 1310 column oven, a 1110 pump and a 1430 diode array detector (Hitachi, Japan).

Generally, to achieve chiral recognition, enantiomers need to be subjected to an asymmetric chiral environment which promotes stereoselective interactions [26–28]. Inspired from previous references [22,29], the chiral precursors are needed to synthesize CCDs and achieve the chiral recognition performance. Previously reported CCDs were synthesized by only one chiral precursor, we also prepared one kind of CCDs using L-Cys as chiral source [24]. We think the more enantioselective sites may lead to more exact caves to match with enantiomers, thus larger difference between enantiomers. Hence, two L-amino acids are chosen as chiral sources to construct CCDs, hoping to achieve stronger enantioselectivity.

Due to the fact that L-Cys is considered a good chiral source for synthesizing CCDs [21,24], it is chosen as one of the carbon sources. One of other 18 amino acids is chosen as another carbon source. The synthetic method of all the CDs is same. After a series of experiments, it is found that only one type of CDs has chiral recognition performance for Trp, and the specific synthesis process of CCDs is as follows. First, 0.5 g L-Cys and 0.25 g L-Lys are dissolved in 80 mL ultrapure water with the help of ultrasound. Then the solution is transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution is filtered through a nylon membrane (220 nm) to remove large particles. The filtrate is put into a dialysis bag (1000 Da) and dialyzed for 3 days. Finally, CCDs are obtained by freeze drying.

The chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers is carried on by fluorescence spectra. The operation process is as simple as follows. 100 μL of CCDs solution (0.5 mg mL-1) are mixed with different concentrations of L- or D-Trp solution, and the total volume of solution is diluted to 5 mL with phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). After 2 min, the solution is put into a fluorescence spectrometer and the fluorescence spectra under 340 nm excitation are measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and detection of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk powder for special medical purposes with deep hydrolysis formula of lactoprotein (ALFARE, Nestle) is used as real sample for determining L-Trp. The pretreatment process of milk powder is as follows. 10 g milk powder is dissolved by moderate deionized water and completely transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask, then 10 mL of zinc acetate solution (220 g/L) is added to above volumetric flask, finally the volume of solution is supplemented to 100 mL by deionized water. After 15 min, the proteins are almost precipitated completely. The solution is filtered for usage. For the detection of L-Trp in milk powder, 1 mL of above solution is mixed with 100 μL of CCDs solution (0.5 mg mL-1), and the volume of solution is diluted to 5 mL with phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). After 2 min, the solution is put into a fluorescence spectrometer and the fluorescence spectra (λex = 340 nm) are measured.

The morphology of CCDs is observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the synthesized CCDs are dispersed spherical nanoparticles. The statistical distribution of particle sizes is obtained by measuring 200 particles in the TEM pictures, it is found that the sizes of CCDs are in the range of 2–8 nm, with an average size of 3.73 nm. The polydispersity of the prepared CCDs solution was investigated by DLS. The average hydrodynamic size of CCDs was 6.5 nm (Fig. S1), indicating good water dispersibility. The larger particle size obtained via DLS is normally expected, which is explained on the basis of formation of electrical double layer on the particle, as well as the path hindrance created with overlapped nanoparticles while conducting the scattering experiment [30]. The chemical composition of CCDs is characterized by FTIR and XPS. Fig. 1b shows the FTIR spectrum of CCDs. The adsorption peak at 3402 cm−1 is possibly derived from the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H chemical bonds. The peak at 3130 cm−1 may come from the stretching vibration of C–H bond in H–C=C–H structure. The peak at 2620 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption peak of S–H. The strong absorption peaks at 1608 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibration of C=N and C–N, respectively. The peak at 1118 cm−1 stems from the stretching vibration of C–O. From above analysis, we can roughly judge that there are amino, hydroxyl, mercapto groups, aromatic and N, S-doping structure in as-synthesized CCDs. The XPS full spectrum of CCDs (Fig. 1c) presents four peaks at 283.8 eV, 399.2 eV, 530.4 eV and 162.7 eV, which are ascribed to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, respectively. The C, N, O and S elements account for 71.5 %, 6.1 %, 17.8 % and 4.6 % in CCDs. The C1s spectrum of CCDs (Fig. 1d) can be divided into four peaks at 283.6 eV, 284.1 eV, 285.2 eV and 287.2 eV, which can be attributed to C=C, C–C, C–N/C–O and C=O/C=N, respectively. The O1s spectrum of the CCDs (Fig. 1e) splits into two peaks at 530.4 eV and 532.0 eV, indicating the presence of C–O and C=O. The N1s spectrum of the CCDs (Fig. 1f) can be divided into two peaks which well-fitted into pyrrolic N (398.9 eV) and graphite N (400.4 eV). The S2p spectrum of the CCDs (Fig. 1g) reveals the existence of S–H (162.6 eV) and C–S (163.8 eV). The XPS data further demonstrates that the CCDs have a carbon-based skeleton and a variety of functional groups containing oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur.

The chiroptical properties of CCDs are studied by circular dichroism spectra. Fig. 1h presents the circular dichroism spectra of L-Lys, L-Cys and CCDs, from which we see two signals centered at 205 nm and 240 nm in the circular dichroism spectrum of CCDs. The signal centered at 205 nm can also be found in the circular dichroism spectrum of L-Lys and L-Cys, which indicates the chiral structure in CCDs presented by the signal at 205 nm is derived from the chiral precursors. The particular signal at 240 nm demonstrates the new chiral center of CCDs, which may be derived from the π–π∗ conjugation of the sp2-hybridized carbon network in CCDs [31]. Moreover, the chiral configuration of this new chiral center is opposite with that of chiral precursors since their circular dichroism signals are contrary. The optical properties of CCDs are investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Fig. 1i shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CCDs, there are three absorption peaks centered at 200 nm, 250 nm and 350 nm, respectively. The absorption band at 250 nm can be attributed to π–π∗ transition of conjugated C=C structure in CCDs. The absorption band at 350 nm originates from the n–π∗ transition of the surface functional groups such as carboxyl and amide. The strong absorption peak at 200 nm is due to the residual groups of L-Cys and L-Lys precursors in the surface of CCDs due to incomplete carbonization. The fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 2 a. We see there are two emission peaks under excitation even if the two emission peaks partially coincide. When the excitation wavelength changes from 320 nm to 380 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of the two peaks red shifts, indicating the excitation dependent emission characteristic of CCDs. The photoluminescence stability of CCDs is tested. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity remains basically unchanged and retains 97 % after continuous exposure to irradiation for 60 min, showing good photobleaching resistance. The CCDs solution is stable for more than 6 months when stored at 0–4 °C, demonstrating a high shelf life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enantioselectivity of CCDs for Trp enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: The chiral recognition property of CCDs for Trp enantiomers is displayed by fluorescence emission spectrum. As shown in Fig. 2b, CCDs have two emission peaks at 390 nm and 450 nm under excitation at 340 nm. In the presence of L-Trp, the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm enhances obviously which is far greater than that at 450 nm. With the increase of the concentration of L-Trp, the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm increases significantly. While on the addition of D-Trp, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm enhances slightly and greater than that at 390 nm. The emission peak at 390 nm can be called the characteristic signal peak of L-Trp. Similarly, the emission peak at 450 nm can be considered as the characteristic signal peak of D-Trp. According to the variation of fluorescence intensity at 390 nm and 450 nm, we can simply distinguish L-Trp and D-Trp. The enantioselectivity (IL/ID, IL is the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm in the presence of L-Trp, and ID is the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm in the presence of D-Trp) reaches 4.5 at the concentration of 1 mM of Trp enantiomer. Comparing the existing chiral recognition methods judging from the variation of fluorescence intensity at the same emission wavelength, the developed chiral recognition method based on the variation of fluorescence intensity at different emission wavelengths is more intuitive and obvious. The fluorescence enhancement mechanism can be explained by the chiral matching interaction between L-Trp or D-Trp and CCDs which restricts intramolecular vibration and rotation of functionalized units in CCDs and reduces the non-radiative relaxation of excited states. As for the fluorescence signal difference between L-Trp or D-Trp, it roots in their different stereoscopic interactions with CCDs.

To establish an optimum detection method, some conditions such as the dosage of CCDs, medium pH and incubation time should be optimized. First, the influence of incubation time between CCDs and Trp enantiomers on the enantioselectivity is investigated. The enantioselectivity (IL/ID) under different incubation time is recorded as shown in Fig. S3. The chiral sensing process is fast since after 2 min of incubation time, the enantioselectivity reaches the maximum. With the extension of incubation time, the enantioselectivity remains unchanged. The fast chiral recognition performance may be due to the rapid mass transfer equilibrium of Trp enantiomers from solution to the surface of CCDs benefited from the good water solubility and biological compatibility of CCDs. Thus, Trp enantiomers can be recognized immediately after mixed well with CCDs. To unify the detection process, the incubation time is set to be 2 min. The influence of pH on the fluorescence property of CCDs and enantioselectivity is explored. When the pH of CCDs solution changes from 2.0 to 9.0, the fluorescence intensity of CCDs increases gradually (Fig. S4). This may be due to the change of the surface charge state of CCDs. While the influence of pH on the enantioselectivity is insignificant (Fig. S5). Considering the applications of probes in biosensing, the physiological pH value (pH = 7.4) is selected as the measurement condition. Finally, the concentration of CCDs is optimized. It is found that with the increase of the concentration of CCDs from 0.05 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL, the enantioselectivity (IL/ID) increases gradually to a maximum at 0.5 mg/mL and then remains unchanged (Fig. S6). Thus, the dosage of CCDs is set to be 0.5 mg/mL in the subsequent experiments.

The change rule of fluorescence intensity in the presence of D- or L-Trp is explored. As shown in Fig. 3 a, with the concentration of L-Trp increases from 0.1 mM to 14 mM, the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm enhances gradually. It is found that the enhancement value of fluorescence intensity (I–I0, I is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of L-Trp, I0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of L-Trp) has good linear relationships with the concentration of L-Trp. As shown in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity first dramatically enhances with the increase of the c(L-Trp), then enhances slowly as the concentration continues to increase. This may be due to that with the concentration of L-Trp increases to a certain extent, the matching efficiency between L-Trp and binding sites on CCDs decreases considering the less spare binding sites and larger steric hindrance, thus the trend of fluorescence enhancement slows down within the specified incubation time. In the concentration range of 0.1–3.7 mM, the quantitative relationship can be expressed by fitted linear equation which is I–I0 = 408.44c + 9.14, R2 = 0.9965. In the range of 3.7–14 mM, the fitted linear equation is I–I0 = 80.97c + 1292.89, R2 = 0.9881. The LOD reaches 5 μM according to 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation and k is the slope of calibration plot. When the concentration of D-Trp increases from 0.2 mM to 16 mM, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm enhances slightly (Fig. 3b). The enhancement value of fluorescence intensity (I–I0) has a good linear relationship with the concentration of D-Trp in the range of 0.2–8.0 mM. The fitted standard curve is I–I0 = 12.42c + 3.74, R2 = 0.9904. The LOD reaches 0.16 mM.

The variation of fluorescence intensity in the presence of racemates with different enantiomeric ratios is also investigated. When D-Trp and L-Trp (the concentration of Trp racemates is 1 mM) coexist in the solution of CCDs, the fluorescence spectra are recorded. As shown in Fig. 3d, with the increase of the percentage of L-Trp in the racemates, the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm enhances obviously. The fluorescence intensity difference (I390–I450, I390 is the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm and I450 is the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm) has a good linear relationship with the proportion of L-Trp (Fig. 3e). The fitted standard curve can be expressed by I390–I450 = 412.23L-Trp% −35.79, R2 = 0.9992. The developed chiral sensing platform not only can be used for quantitative analysis of D-Trp and L-Trp, but also can be used for determining the enantiomeric excess of racemates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific chiral recognition ability for Trp enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: The enantioselectivity (IL/ID) of CCDs towards various chiral amino acids is investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3f, there is no significant difference of fluorescent signal between L and D isomers of another 18 chiral amino acids in the solution of CCDs. Only Trp enantiomers can be chiral recognized whose IL/ID reaches 4.5 at the same concentration with others. Experimental results show that the prepared CCDs have specific chiral recognition performance for Trp enantiomers, which may be due to that the stereo space formed in CCDs precisely matches with Trp. The effect of common substances such as carbohydrates, vitamins and ions on the enantioselectivity of CCDs was investigated (Fig. S7). On the addition of common interfering substances at a concentration of 100 times than Trp (0.2 mM), the fluorescence intensity of the two emission peaks changed a little, indicating the detection system had good anti-interference ability.

A possible molecular fragment of the prepared CCDs was constructed according to the structure characteristics of CCDs and previously reported article [21,32]. The structure of CCDs was optimized by molecular dynamics simulation and energy minimization using software of Chem 3D 20.0. Molecular docking between CCDs and D-/L-Trp was carried on using software of AutoDock 4.2.6. Fig. 4 shows the optimized fragmented structure of CCDs and three interaction modes between CCDs and D-/L-Trp. Chiral recognition ultimately relies on different interaction forces. Molecular docking was applied to study the interactions between D-/L-Trp and CCDs. There are different types of chiral geometries in chiral nanomaterials, chirality of nanostructures could be found at atomic, nanoscale and submicron scale [33]. It is significant to identify what chiral geometry is responsible for the chiral recognition. From the molecular docking model, it is found that the chiral recognition sites are concentrated in the residual chiral groups on the edge of CCDs. No obvious nanoscale chiral geometries are identified. We speculate that it is the chirality at atomic scale resulting in the chiral recognition. From the docking results we see D-Trp and L-Trp forms π-π interaction and hydrogen bond interaction with CCDs. The aromatic rings of Trp form π-π interaction with the large conjugated system of CCDs, which restricts the variation of molecular conformation to some extent. The carboxyl and amino groups of Trp form hydrogen bond interaction with the residues of CCDs, the strength and orientation of hydrogen bonds between D-/L-Trp and CCDs seems the key to achieve stereoscopic recognition. The chiral residues of CCDs provide a strict three-dimensional cavity, L-Trp has stronger matching ability with the cavity according to the distance of hydrogen bonds and the binding energy. See in Fig. 4, the distance of hydrogen bonds between L-Trp and CCDs is shorter than that of D-Trp and CCDs, meaning stronger interaction between L-Trp and CCDs, the binding energy data listed in Table S1 verified this conclusion. We also calculated the binding constant (k) between D-/L-Trp and CCDs. The change in the fluorescence intensity value of CCDs by adding different concentrations of L-Trp or D-Trp is analyzed using the Benesi–Hildebrand relation [Eq. (1)] [34,35].
Eq. (1)
1
|
F −
F 0
|
=
1
k c
|
F max
−
F 0
|
+
1
|
F max
−
F 0
|
Where k is the binding constant, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CCDs in the absence and presence of Trp, Fmax is the maximum value that fluorescence intensity can reach in the presence of Trp. The plot of 1/|I–I0| versus 1/c fitted to Benesi–Hildebrand relation was found to be linear with good correlation coefficient and is shown in Fig. S8. From the slope and intercept of this plot, the association constant (k) value is calculated. It was found that L-Trp with k of 129 M−1 had stronger interaction than D-Trp with k of 92 M−1. The differences of Trp enantiomers in association constant obtained from experimental data is in accord with the differences in binding energy obtained by computer simulation, which supports the conclusion that stronger interaction exists between L-Trp and CCDs. It is precisely this slight difference in binding ability that makes chiral recognition possible.

As an essential amino acid, L-Trp is usually employed as a supplement in human nutrition. Therefore, it is of great significance to monitor the content of L-Trp in foods. Here, the developed chiral sensing platform is applied for detecting L-Trp in milk powder for special medical purposes with deep hydrolysis formula of lactoprotein. The detection results are verified by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). It is found that the detection results using this method are in accord with that obtained by HPLC. The standard addition method is further used to verify the reliability of the developed method and the results are summarized in Table S2. The satisfactory recoveries of 93.3%–103 % and RSDs below 6 % illustrate that the proposed sensing method has excellent accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of this method with others
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method is compared with current chiral recognition methods based on fluorescence detection for Trp. As shown is Table 1 , comparing with organic molecules, enzyme-loaded complex or semiconductor quantum dots as probes, the chiral recognition probe in this method are green nanomaterials with simpler preparation method. Moreover, the chiral recognition process of this method is fast and simple without needing metal ions as sensing medium. It is worth noting that this chiral sensing system not only achieves the signal difference between L-Trp and D-Trp in terms of fluorescence intensity, but also achieves the distinguishment of Trp enantiomers in terms of emission wavelength, which is a progress in the field of fluorescent chiral recognition.

In this work, novel CCDs are synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method using L-Lys and L-Cys as precursors. The CCDs have a carbon-based skeleton and a variety of functional groups containing oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur. The CCDs show two fluorescence emission peaks at 390 nm and 450 nm, respectively. It is a pity that there is a significant overlap between the two emission peaks. The fluorescence intensity of CCDs at 390 nm enhances obviously on the addition of L-Trp, while it enhances slightly at 450 nm in the presence of D-Trp. Trp enantiomers can be recognized based on the fluorescence intensity change at different emission peaks. The enantioselectivity (IL/ID) reaches 4.5 when the concentration of Trp enantiomer is 1.0 mM. The developed chiral sensing platform not only can be used for quantitative analysis of D-Trp and L-Trp, but also can be used for determining the enantiomeric excess of racemates. The accurate detection results and satisfactory recovery rates for detecting L-Trp in milk powder verified the reliability of this method. This chiral sensing system not only can identify Trp enantiomers according to fluorescence intensity difference, but also achieves the distinguishment depending on emission peak positions. We have to admit that the serious overlap of the two emission peaks results in the distinguishment of enantiomers relying on emission wavelengths not very ideal. However, this work opens the door to identify chiral isomers according to wavelength difference, which has profound significance for the development of chiral sensing platforms.

Qi Wu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Yuan Chen: Investigation. Yan-Li Wang: Methodology. Ji-Ying Song: Software. Hai-Tao Lv: Supervision, Validation. Ya-Ming Sun: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2024MB097), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 22206044), the High-Level Talents Scientific Research Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016).

ce:section.ce:para:
